title: Culling the Masses: The Democratic Origins of Racist Immigration Policy in the Americas By David Scott FitzGerald and David Cook-Martín Harvard University Press. 2014. 512 pages. $55 hardcover
abstract: The dust jacket of Culling the Masses proclaims that the book “questions the widely held view that in the long run democracy and racism cannot coexist.” After reading that, I opened this hefty book prepared for a straw-man thesis. There may be some naïve enough to believe that democracy and racial exclusiveness are inconsistent, but I think most people who have any knowledge of history or political theory are familiar with the concept of the tyranny of the majority, which dates back at least to the Federalist Papers, and with commonplace modern applications of that concept to majority and minority racial groups. Although the authors do claim that conventional wisdom holds that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with racial exclusion, their work offers more than that simplistic and misleading assertion. The book is actually a detailed and extensive examination of policies of racial and ethnic exclusion in many of the nations throughout the Americas and an effort to explain the motivations for exclusive policies and the reasons for a general movement away from them.
